structured_choices.data_source: Production e-commerce platform session logs spanning 24 months. Raw data includes: timestamped events (queries, clicks, add-to-cart, purchases, dwell time in milliseconds), user identifiers (anonymized), item identifiers with catalog metadata (category, price, seller, historical CTR), session identifiers, ranking position at which each item was shown, and item display status (visible above fold, below fold). Data is partitioned by calendar date and session ID. Time period is chosen to include at least one full seasonal cycle and multiple promotion calendars to ensure representativeness.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary relevance labels are derived from implicit feedback within each session, defined as: label=1 if user clicked on item AND (dwell_time > 3 seconds OR item was added-to-cart OR item was purchased within the session); label=0 otherwise. This multi-signal label balances engagement depth with commercial value. Labels are assigned at the session-item-position tuple level: for each (query, session, item, position) presented to the user, we assign the label based on the user's response to that specific exposure. Negative examples include all shown items that did not receive engagement.
structured_choices.split_method: Chronological session-level split over time to prevent label leakage and respect temporal dependencies. Train set: calendar months 1–16 (67% of data, ~16 months). Validation set: months 17–20 (17% of data, ~4 months). Test set: months 21–24 (16% of data, ~4 months). Split is applied at the session granularity: if a session spans a split boundary, the entire session is assigned to the earlier split. This preserves session coherence and prevents information from future sessions influencing training. Justification: (1) Learning-to-rank models exhibit strong temporal dependencies—a user's click behavior on day 1 of a session should not influence ranking policy training for that same session. (2) Chronological splits simulate the real deployment scenario where the model must rank items for future, unseen sessions. (3) Random splits would introduce look-ahead bias by allowing post-interaction events to influence feature construction for early-session items.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (feature scalers, categorical encoders, imputation strategies, and sequence normalizers) are fit exclusively on the training set (months 1–16). These transformers are then applied deterministically (without refitting) to validation and test sets. Specifically: (1) Min-max scalers for numeric features (dwell time, item price, historical CTR) are computed from training data only. (2) Vocabulary for categorical encoders (query tokens, category IDs, seller IDs) is learned from training set; OOV items in val/test are mapped to an <UNK> token. (3) Query tokenization and sequence padding lengths are determined from the training set's empirical distribution of query lengths. (4) Any session-level aggregations (e.g., user's average click rate in the session so far) are computed in a causal manner: features for position t in a session use only data from positions 1 to t−1.
structured_choices.features: Two feature groups are constructed: (1) Sequence features: raw clickstream encoded as a sequence of (event_type, item_id, dwell_time, timestamp_delta) tuples, truncated/padded to length 50 (95th percentile of session length). Event types are one-hot encoded. (2) Context features: (query embedding via pre-trained fastText on historical queries, query length, session length so far, time-of-day, day-of-week, promoted_item flag, item's category, item's price quantile within category, item's historical CTR in the training set, item's seller reputation score). No user ID is directly included; instead, user behavioral patterns are captured through the sequence history. All features respect temporal causality: features for ranking position t do not include information from positions t+1 onwards.
structured_choices.model: Bidirectional LSTM (or Transformer encoder) with attention over the clickstream sequence, followed by a context-aware ranking head. Architecture: (1) Sequence encoder: BiLSTM with 128 hidden units, dropout=0.3, processes raw clickstream events. (2) Context projection: feedforward network embeds context features (query, item, session features) into 64-dim vectors. (3) Attention layer: multiplicative attention between the sequence hidden states and the candidate item's context embedding. (4) Ranking head: feedforward MLP (2 layers, 128→64→1) that produces a relevance score. Sigmoid activation produces predicted probability of click/engagement. Model is trained end-to-end on the training set using Adam optimizer (learning rate 1e-3), early stopping on validation set primary metric (see below), batch size 32, max epochs 50.
structured_choices.baseline: A learning-to-rank model using only aggregated user and item features, with equivalent architecture and tuning effort. Baseline model: (1) Removes the sequence encoder and attention mechanism entirely. (2) Uses the same context features (query embedding, item features, session features) as the sequence model. (3) Employs a feedforward ranking head (3 layers, 256→128→64→1) with dropout=0.3, trained with identical optimizer and early stopping. The baseline's feature set is a strict subset of the sequence model's features (no sequence history). Both models receive equal hyperparameter search effort: 20 random trials on the validation set for learning rate, dropout, hidden layer sizes. Baseline tuning uses the same validation set and primary metric. This ensures the comparison isolates the benefit of sequence modeling rather than architecture or tuning differences.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain at k=5 (NDCG@5), computed per session, then averaged across all test-set sessions. NDCG@5 is chosen because: (1) It directly aligns with stakeholder intent: showing relevant items early in the ranking matters most for CTR and revenue. Discounting by position reflects user attention decay. (2) It is position-aware, accounting for the fact that items shown first are clicked more often (position bias inherent in the data). (3) It uses the relevance labels (clicks + engagement signals) as ground truth, avoiding synthetic metrics disconnected from business outcomes. (4) For each session, we compute: DCG@5 = Σ(rel_i / log₂(i+1)) for top-5 ranked items, then normalize by ideal DCG@5 computed from the session's actual relevant items sorted in descending order of engagement signal strength. This is session-specific normalization, which handles variable session lengths and relevance distributions.
secondary_metrics: Recall@5: fraction of relevant items (from implicit labels) present in the top 5, averaged across sessions. Complementary to NDCG; measures coverage.
Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR): position of the first relevant item in the ranking, averaged across sessions. Sensitive to early precision.
Precision@5: fraction of top-5 items that are relevant, controlling for false-positive rankings.

Propensity-adjusted AUC: AUC computed with inverse-propensity weighting (IPW) on test-set predictions, using position-exposure propensity scores estimated from the training set to reduce position-bias artifacts. This secondary metric checks whether improvements are robust to exposure bias.
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning and model selection is performed exclusively on the validation set (months 17–20). For each candidate hyperparameter configuration, the model is trained on the training set and evaluated on the validation set using the primary metric (NDCG@5). The configuration achieving the highest validation NDCG@5 is selected. Early stopping is also performed on validation NDCG@5: training halts if validation NDCG@5 does not improve for 5 consecutive epochs. The test set (months 21–24) is used only once, at the end, to report final performance numbers. No tuning or model selection occurs on the test set.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is held completely untouched until final evaluation. After both the sequence model and baseline are fully trained, tuned, and frozen using only training and validation data, they are evaluated on the test set once. Test results are reported for all primary and secondary metrics. No retuning, retraining, or threshold adjustment is performed after test evaluation. This one-touch policy ensures the test set serves as a true held-out benchmark and prevents overfitting to the test distribution.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Four confounds are explicitly managed: (1) Position bias and exposure bias: The baseline and sequence model are both trained on implicit feedback from the same logged ranking policy. However, we compute a secondary metric (propensity-adjusted AUC) that applies inverse-propensity weighting to the test predictions, using position exposure propensity scores estimated from the training set. Improvements that persist under IPW adjustment are more likely to reflect genuine relevance gains rather than policy artifacts. (2) Popularity bias: Both models have access to item popularity features (historical CTR). We report held-out performance per item popularity quartile to check whether gains are uniform or concentrated in popular items, which would suggest the sequence model merely memorizes popularity rather than learning session dynamics. (3) Seasonality and catalog changes: The 24-month training and test windows span multiple seasons and promotion campaigns. No season-specific retraining is performed; if the sequence model generalizes, it must handle calendar variation within a single model. Post-hoc, we stratify test results by season (Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4 within the test window) to verify performance consistency. (4) Query-specific effects: We report NDCG@5 separately for query frequency quartiles (high-frequency queries vs. rare queries) to verify that improvements are not driven only by common queries. If the sequence model excels only on popular queries and degrades on rare ones, this indicates insufficient generalization.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Sequence Models for E-Commerce Product Ranking

### 1. Objective and Hypothesis
We hypothesize that a sequence model trained on raw clickstream and session-event data will outrank a model using only aggregated user and item features in predicting product relevance and improving click-through and conversion on held-out sessions. The evaluation focuses on ranking quality for unseen user sessions in a real e-commerce environment.

### 2. Data Strategy
We use 24 months of production session logs from the e-commerce platform, providing approximately 5–10 million sessions with full event-level granularity. For each session, we capture timestamped events (queries, clicks, add-to-cart, purchases, dwell time), item identifiers with metadata (category, price, seller, historical engagement), and ranking positions shown to users.

Labels are derived from implicit feedback: an item is marked relevant (label=1) if the user clicked on it AND spent >3 seconds viewing it OR added it to cart OR purchased it within the session. This multi-signal definition balances engagement depth with commercial intent. Negative examples are all shown items without these engagement signals.

Known data quality issues are addressed as follows: (1) Missing catalog metadata is imputed using the mode (category) or median (price) within category, fitted on training data only. (2) Sessions with incomplete or malformed event sequences are filtered (estimated <2% of data). (3) Catalog changes (new items, discontinued items) are handled by flagging items as "new" if they first appear after month 16, allowing the model to learn a separate representation for new items.

### 3. Split Strategy
We employ a chronological session-level split to prevent label leakage and respect temporal dependencies inherent in session data:
- **Training set:** Months 1–16 (67%, ~16 months of data)
- **Validation set:** Months 17–20 (17%, ~4 months)
- **Test set:** Months 21–24 (16%, ~4 months)

Each session is assigned entirely to the earliest split it spans. This prevents future user behavior from influencing model training on earlier sessions and simulates the real deployment scenario where the model ranks items for future sessions it has not seen.

### 4. Feature Engineering
Features are constructed in two groups:

**Sequence features:** Raw clickstream encoded as a sequence of (event_type, item_id, dwell_time, timestamp_delta) tuples, truncated or padded to 50 tokens (95th percentile session length). Event types (click, add-to-cart, purchase, view) are one-hot encoded.

**Context features:** Query embedding (pre-trained fastText), query length, session length so far, temporal features (hour-of-day, day-of-week), promoted item flag, item category, item price quantile within category, item's historical CTR (fitted on training set), and seller reputation. User ID is not directly included; user patterns are captured through session history.

All preprocessing transformers (scalers, encoders, vocabulary) are fit on the training set only and applied deterministically to validation and test sets. Features respect temporal causality: at ranking position t, no information from positions t+1 onwards is included.

### 5. Model and Baseline
**Sequence Model:** A bidirectional LSTM (128 hidden units, dropout=0.3) encodes the raw clickstream. A multiplicative attention layer weights the sequence hidden states by the candidate item's context embedding (64-dim). A 2-layer ranking MLP (128→64→1) produces relevance scores. The model is trained end-to-end with Adam (lr=1e-3), early stopping on validation NDCG@5, batch size 32, max 50 epochs.

**Baseline Model:** An identical architecture but without sequence encoding or attention. Instead, it takes the context features directly into a 3-layer ranking MLP (256→128→64→1) with dropout=0.3, trained with the same optimizer and early stopping criterion. This baseline is a valid learning-to-rank model using only aggregated features—a fair comparison because both models receive equal tuning effort (20 random hyperparameter trials on validation data) and identical computational resources.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics
**Primary metric:** Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain at k=5 (NDCG@5), computed per session and averaged across the test set. We choose NDCG@5 because it aligns with stakeholder objectives (showing relevant items early matters most for CTR and revenue), is position-aware, and uses engagement labels as ground truth. Session-level normalization handles variable relevance distributions.

**Secondary metrics:** Recall@5 (coverage of relevant items), Mean Reciprocal Rank (early precision), Precision@5 (false-positive control), and propensity-adjusted AUC (robustness to position and exposure bias).

### 7. Validation and Model Selection
Both models are tuned on the validation set only. For each hyperparameter candidate, the model is trained on training data and evaluated on validation data using NDCG@5. The best configuration is selected. The test set is held untouched until final evaluation, at which point each frozen model is evaluated once on all metrics and reported.

### 8. Controls for Confounds
We manage four key confounds:
- **Position and exposure bias:** Propensity-adjusted AUC (via IPW, propensity from training set) checks whether gains persist when position effects are removed.
- **Popularity bias:** Stratified test results by item popularity quartile ensure gains are not merely memorization of popular items.
- **Seasonality:** Test results are stratified by quarter within the test window to verify generalization across seasons.
- **Query-specific effects:** NDCG@5 is reported separately for query frequency quartiles, confirming improvements generalize to rare queries as well.

This design establishes whether sequence models genuinely capture session dynamics beyond aggregated statistics and position exposure, or whether improvements are artifacts of feedback loops and popularity bias.
